title: Case 001: Pelican to Murder
description: A live bar mystery where every player is a Detective investigating a death during a packed theme night at Pelican to Mars.

answerKey.method: Switched Lena’s allergy-safe drink and hid her injector
answerKey.motive: To hide the stolen sponsor money before Lena exposed him
answerKey.keyEvidence: Camera export gap, safe access log, fake sponsor invoices, and Owen’s false timeline
answerKey.explanation: Owen Landry had the motive, access, method, and cover-up trail. Lena had discovered the fake sponsor payments. Owen could access the service route, manipulate the camera export, switch the marked allergy-safe drink, and hide the injector in the office safe using his manager code.

publicClues.title: Case Opened
publicClues.text: Detectives are called to Pelican to Mars after Lena Broussard, the host of the night’s murder mystery launch, collapses before the final toast. The venue is secured, the guests are held for statements, and all evidence will release through the shared investigation feed.

publicClues.title: Scene Split
publicClues.text: The initial scene has three areas of interest: the main bar where Lena received her drink, the patio corridor where she collapsed, and the manager office where several event documents were kept.

publicClues.title: Medical Detail
publicClues.text: Witnesses confirm Lena carried an emergency injector because of a severe allergy. Her purse was recovered near the host table, but the injector was not inside it.

publicClues.title: False Simplicity
publicClues.text: At first glance, the death could be blamed on a drink mistake. Detectives note that a simple bar error would not explain the missing injector, the edited camera export, or the sponsor files removed from the office printer.

publicClues.title: Final Mystery Questions Open
publicClues.text: The final accusation is open. Complete all 10 mystery questions: five checkpoint answers and five final accusation answers.

phone.title: Victim Call Log
phone.text: Lena missed two calls from Owen Landry at 7:41 PM and 7:44 PM. A third call at 7:46 PM lasted only nine seconds, shortly before she returned to the host table.

phone.title: Deleted Draft Fragment
phone.text: A recovered draft on Lena’s phone reads: 'If Owen won’t fix the sponsor money tonight, I’ll say it before the toast. I kept copies.' The message was never sent.

phone.title: Manager Callback
phone.text: Owen called Lena again after the collapse, while standing in the manager office according to the phone location record. Detectives flag the call because Lena’s phone was already in police custody by then.

messages.title: Bartender Text Thread
messages.text: Tessa, the bartender, texted Lena at 6:58 PM: 'I marked yours no-praline like always. Don’t let anybody grab the wrong pelican glass.' The tone reads protective, not hostile.

messages.title: Sound Booth Argument
messages.text: Cole, the sound tech, texted a friend that Lena was 'about to ruin the night with a speech.' Detectives note the message sounds angry, but it does not mention money, drinks, or the missing injector.

messages.title: Sponsor Warning
messages.text: A sponsor text to Lena says, 'Owen sent us a second invoice. That can’t be right, can it?' Lena replied, 'No. Save everything. I’ll handle it tonight.'

maps.title: Patio Corridor Ping
maps.text: Lena’s phone moved from the host table to the patio corridor at 8:07 PM. Owen’s phone appeared in the same corridor for less than one minute before returning to the manager office.

maps.title: Office Access Path
maps.text: The manager office sits behind the bar, but it can also be reached through the patio corridor. This route avoids the main bar camera for a short stretch.

bank.title: Duplicate Sponsor Deposit
bank.text: Lena’s shared event ledger shows one sponsor deposit marked paid. A separate bank memo from the same sponsor appears under a private vendor label controlled by Owen.

bank.title: Refund Panic
bank.text: Four refund drafts were created from Owen’s account but never sent. The drafts match the sponsor amounts Lena had circled in her ledger.

photos.title: Two Pelican Glasses
photos.text: A guest photo shows two identical Pelican to Mars glasses on the service rail. One has a small blue pick, which Tessa says marked Lena’s allergy-safe drink. The glass Lena received in a later photo has no blue pick.

photos.title: Office Printer Photo
photos.text: A detective photo of the office printer tray shows a torn corner from a sponsor invoice. The remaining page edge matches the paper size used in Owen’s private vendor folder.

photos.title: Drawer Reflection
photos.text: A photo taken after the collapse catches the manager desk drawer slightly open in a mirror reflection. The drawer was later found locked when police arrived.

social.title: Influencer Clip
social.text: Brielle’s livestream clip shows Owen stepping behind the bar while Tessa turns to answer a guest. The clip cuts off before the drink is carried away.

social.title: Deleted Story Caption
social.text: A deleted story from Lena’s account was scheduled but never posted. The caption reads: 'Tonight’s real mystery is where the sponsor money went.'

contacts.title: Private Vendor Contact
contacts.text: Owen’s phone lists a contact named 'Mars Event Services.' The number traces back to a prepaid line used to collect sponsor payments, not a real vendor office.

contacts.title: Emergency Contact Note
contacts.text: Lena’s emergency note says her injector stays in the front pocket of her purse unless the manager locks valuables in the office safe. Only managers had the safe code.

notes.title: Detective Scene Note
notes.text: The blue-pick allergy marker matters because staff used it for Lena’s drinks all night. Any suspect who knew the marker system could make the switch look accidental.

notes.title: Victim Notebook
notes.text: Lena’s notebook includes three initials beside sponsor amounts. The initials O.L. are circled twice, with the note: 'Ask before toast. Do not let him stall again.'

notes.title: Red Herring Review
notes.text: Cole had a public argument with Lena, and Brielle’s clip was edited for drama, but neither had office access, sponsor account control, or a reason to hide the injector.

files.title: Camera Export Gap
files.text: The security export skips from 8:05:12 PM to 8:07:44 PM. The file properties show the export was created from Owen’s manager login.

files.title: Sponsor Invoice Comparison
files.text: The official sponsor invoice and the private vendor invoice share the same typo in 'entertainment.' Detectives treat the typo as a signature mistake linking both documents.

files.title: Safe Access Log
files.text: The office safe was opened at 8:08 PM with Owen’s manager code. The missing injector was recovered inside the safe after the reveal search.

browser.title: Search History
browser.text: Owen’s browser history includes searches about removing metadata from exported camera clips. The searches occurred two days before the event.

browser.title: Vendor Site Lookup
browser.text: The fake vendor name 'Mars Event Services' was searched from the manager office computer before the sponsor invoices were created.

rounds.title: Round 1: The Body
rounds.objective: Review the opening scene, the victim’s collapse, and the first suspicious details. Answer checkpoint 1 after this round.
rounds.dialogue: Detectives, Round 1 begins now. Lena Broussard is dead, and the room wants you to believe it was chaos, bad luck, and a misplaced drink. Do not accept that story. Focus on the body, the drink exchange, and the exact place where she collapsed. The first lie is already sitting in plain sight.
rounds.countdownReview: Round 1 is ending. Review the scene: Lena’s death looks accidental, but the drink marker, the missing injector, and the collapse location all suggest deliberate interference.

rounds.title: Round 2: The Timeline
rounds.objective: Use calls, messages, and movement records to rebuild who was where and when. Answer checkpoint 2 after this round.
rounds.dialogue: Detectives, Round 2 is live. Now the case shifts from shock to structure. Rebuild the timeline. Who called Lena, who crossed paths with her, and who appeared where they should not have been? The truth is tightening around movement, not rumor.
rounds.countdownReview: Round 2 is ending. Check the timeline again. Calls, texts, and location pings narrowed the field. Someone’s movements no longer match their story.

rounds.title: Round 3: The Digital Trail
rounds.objective: Review camera files, exported clips, searches, and social posts. Answer checkpoint 3 after this round.
rounds.dialogue: Detectives, Round 3 is now open. Digital evidence is never just a screen; it is a trail. Look at what was exported, what was deleted, and who knew how to bend the record.
rounds.countdownReview: Round 3 is ending. The digital trail should show you which evidence was altered and whose access made that possible.

rounds.title: Round 4: The Money
rounds.objective: Follow the sponsor deposits, vendor records, invoices, and refund drafts. Answer checkpoint 4 after this round.
rounds.dialogue: Detectives, Round 4 is live. Motive speaks in numbers tonight. Follow the sponsor money, the vendor names, the duplicate invoice, and the panic hiding inside the paperwork.
rounds.countdownReview: Round 4 is ending. The money trail should now point toward motive and narrow the suspect pool.

rounds.title: Round 5: The Cover-Up
rounds.objective: Connect the method, office access, hidden injector, and cover-up. Answer checkpoint 5 after this round.
rounds.dialogue: Detectives, Round 5 is now open. This is where the cover-up breaks. The killer did not just strike — they tried to write a false ending afterward. Find the link between the safe, the camera gap, the fake vendor, and the missing injector.
rounds.countdownReview: Round 5 is ending. The method, motive, and key evidence should now line up behind one suspect.

rounds.title: Final Accusation
rounds.objective: Submit your final 5 accusation answers and make sure all 10 mystery questions are complete.
rounds.dialogue: Detectives, the final accusation phase is open. You have enough to close the case. Review your five checkpoint answers, then submit the final five questions: culprit, method, motive, proof, and the statement that falls apart.
rounds.countdownReview: The accusation window is nearly closed. Commit to your final theory.

openingNarration: Good evening, Detectives. Welcome to Pelican to Mars. Tonight began as a launch party and ended as a crime scene. Lena Broussard collapsed before the final toast, and what first looked like a tragic mistake is already showing signs of a planned cover-up. You will investigate together. There are no secret roles, no private clue lanes, and no shortcuts. Follow the evidence, question the timeline, and be careful who you trust. The truth is buried across calls, messages, locations, financial records, and the lies people tell when they think the room is too loud for anyone to notice.
revealScript: Detectives, the case is closed. Lena Broussard was murdered by Owen Landry. His motive was simple greed wrapped in panic: Lena had discovered he was skimming sponsor money and preparing to expose him before the final toast. To silence her, he tampered with the drink exchange and then hid her emergency injector to make the death look like a terrible accident. But the cover-up broke apart under pressure. The camera gap, the fake sponsor invoices, the office safe log, and Lena’s unsent warning all point in the same direction. The bar was full of noise, but the timeline never lied.

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 1: What detail at the scene makes Lena’s collapse suspicious?

options.label: Her allergy-safe drink marker changed and her injector was missing
The music stopped during the toast
The bar lights flickered before she fell
The stage was empty when she collapsed

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 2: Which timeline issue creates the first major contradiction?

options.label: Owen’s phone appears near Lena in the patio corridor during the critical window
Cole called Lena earlier in the night
Tessa worked near the service rail
Brielle filmed the party for social media

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 3: Which digital record suggests evidence was altered?

options.label: The security export has a gap and was created from Owen’s manager login
Lena had a scheduled social post
A guest photo shows two glasses
A missed call appears from the bar phone

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 4: What financial clue points toward motive?

options.label: Sponsor money was routed through a fake private vendor connected to Owen
A bartender received a large cash tip
A guest received a free drink
The cover charge was higher than expected

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 5: Which clue connects the cover-up directly to the killer?

options.label: The office safe was opened with Owen’s manager code after the collapse
Cole argued with Lena in public
Brielle deleted a dramatic story
A blue pick marked one drink earlier in the night
Owen Landry
Tessa Marchand
Cole Vinet
Brielle Hart

questions.prompt: Final 2: How was the murder carried out?

options.label: Switched Lena’s allergy-safe drink and hid her injector
Poisoned the bar’s shared punch bowl
Pushed Lena in the patio corridor
Staged a fake overdose with pills
To hide the stolen sponsor money
To win the launch party competition
To cover up a romantic affair
To protect Brielle’s livestream brand

questions.prompt: Final 4: Which evidence best proves the case?

options.label: Camera export gap, safe access log, and fake sponsor invoices
A deleted social story by itself
Cole’s public argument with Lena
A single missed call from Tessa

questions.prompt: Final 5: Which suspect’s statement falls apart when matched against the timeline?

options.label: Owen said he never left the office path during the drink exchange
Tessa said she marked allergy-safe drinks with a blue pick
Cole admitted he argued with Lena earlier
Brielle admitted she filmed clips for attention
